metadata.title: Precognition: Make Claude Think Before It Speaks
sources: Anthropic Prompt Engineering Interactive Tutorial — Lesson 6: Precognition (github.com/anthropics/prompt-eng-interactive-tutorial)

beats.narration_text: Making Claude think before it speaks sounds like magic until I ask why writing out reasoning first changes the final answer at all. Take a word problem where the naive answer is a tempting trap: (1) show me the model answering immediately and getting it wrong, (2) then force step-by-step reasoning before the conclusion and point to the exact step where the trap gets caught, (3) explain whether hiding that reasoning in tags versus showing it changes the accuracy or just the presentation.

props.command: Making Claude think before it speaks sounds like magic until I ask why writing out reasoning first changes the final answer at all. Take a word problem where the naive answer is a tempting trap: (1) show me the model answering immediately and getting it wrong, (2) then force step-by-step reasoning before the conclusion and point to the exact step where the trap gets caught, (3) explain whether hiding that reasoning in tags versus showing it changes the accuracy or just the presentation.
output: Claude generates tokens left-to-right — no backtracking
Intermediate steps in output feed forward into the answer
Thinking only counts when it's out loud
XML scratchpad makes it structured and reliable

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's the failure mode. Ask Claude to classify a sarcastic movie review without thinking steps and it takes the words literally — 'freshness and originality' reads as positive even when the reviewer wrote 'I've been living under a rock since 1900.' Ask Claude to first write arguments for each side in XML tags, then answer — and it gets it right. Same text. Different process.

props.artifactHeading: Direct answer = takes words literally. Thinking first = correct.
artifactLines: Prompt without thinking: 'Is this review positive or negative?'
Result: Claude reads 'freshness and originality' → POSITIVE
Prompt with XML steps: write <positive-argument> and <negative-argument> first
Result: Claude catches the sarcasm → NEGATIVE
Same review. Same model. Different output path.

beats.narration_text: The XML scratchpad pattern: put a thinking block before the answer block. Something like: 'First brainstorm in brainstorm tags, then give your answer.' Claude fills the tags with intermediate reasoning, then the answer comes after — informed by everything that preceded it. You can also use thinking tags, scratchpad tags, or any label. The tag name doesn't matter. The position does.

props.artifactHeading: Position matters. The tag name doesn't.
artifactLines: <brainstorm> ... reasoning ... </brainstorm>  →  final answer
<thinking> ... intermediate steps ... </thinking>  →  answer
<positive-argument> ... </positive-argument> then conclude
Rule: scratchpad BEFORE final output, not after
Works for: classification, fact recall, logic, sentiment

beats.narration_text: There's a subtlety: Claude is sensitive to ordering within the scratchpad. In the lesson, swapping which argument comes first — positive before negative versus negative before positive — changes the final classification. Claude is more likely to agree with whichever side it wrote last. So for ambiguous cases, put the correct framing second. That's not a bug — it's the token sequence doing exactly what it should.

props.artifactHeading: Claude leans toward the argument it wrote last.
artifactLines: <positive-argument> first, <negative-argument> second → leans NEGATIVE
<negative-argument> first, <positive-argument> second → leans POSITIVE
This is token-sequence physics, not a flaw
Fix: put the framing you want to dominate last
Use for ambiguous cases — unambiguous ones aren't affected

beats.narration_text: The principle generalizes. The tutorial calls it precognition — making Claude think before it speaks, the way you'd brief a human consultant. Any time Claude gets a fact wrong or misclassifies something, the first fix isn't a better prompt — it's a thinking step. Ask Claude to brainstorm actors born in 1956 before naming one from a film and the factual accuracy jumps immediately. The reasoning step is load-bearing.

props.artifactHeading: First fix for any wrong answer: add a thinking step.
artifactLines: Prompt: 'Name a movie with an actor born in 1956' → often wrong
Fix: 'Brainstorm actors and birth years in <brainstorm> tags, then answer'
Claude recalls candidates → filters → gives accurate result
Applies to: fact recall, classification, logic, multi-step math
Thinking only counts when it's in the output — not implicit

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Ask Claude: 'Before answering, write your reasoning in scratchpad tags. Then tell me: is the following argument logically valid, and what's the strongest objection to it? Argument: All successful startups have strong founders. This startup has strong founders. Therefore it will succeed.' You're not just testing the logic — you're watching whether the scratchpad catches the base-rate fallacy before the final answer lands. The thinking step is the test. Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: Before answering, write your reasoning in <scratchpad> tags. Is this argument logically valid, and what's the strongest objection? Argument: All successful startups have strong founders. This startup has strong founders. Therefore it will succeed.
props.segment: Precognition: Make Claude Think Before It Speaks
output: Claude reasons through the logical form in the scratchpad
Catches the base-rate fallacy before committing to an answer
Demonstrates that visible thinking changes the final output

beats.narration_text: Precognition: Make Claude Think Before It Speaks
